story.headline: Iranian missile kills two US service members in Jordan; US answers with an eighth night of strikes on Iran
story.summary: An Iranian strike on a US base in Jordan killed two American service members and left a third missing, the first US combat deaths since the April truce, drawing an eighth consecutive night of US strikes on the IRGC.
story.display_body: An Iranian missile killed two US service members at a base in Jordan on Saturday 18 July, drawing an eighth consecutive night of US strikes on Iran. US Central Command (CENTCOM — the American military command for the Middle East) said the strike also left a third soldier missing in action (MIA) and wounded four more; these are the first US combat deaths since a truce with Iran in early April, and they land after a week of the heaviest fighting since the war erupted in February. In response, the US launched its eighth straight night of strikes overnight into Sunday, with CENTCOM saying the latest wave targeted Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC — Iran's elite parallel military). Iran reported strikes in its south and around the Strait of Hormuz, US bases in Kuwait came under fire, and the United Arab Emirates was threatened; Washington warned Iran-backed groups might target Americans abroad and said it was moving more warplanes to the region. Iran's supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, vowed the US would be taught "unforgettable lessons," and Tehran said it had suspended implementation of a memorandum of understanding.
story.why: Direct American casualties cross a line both sides had avoided since April, sharply raising the risk that a contained air campaign widens into a broader regional war astride the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint through which much of the world's seaborne oil passes.

story.headline: Russia launches largest ballistic-missile barrage on Kyiv since the war began
story.summary: Hours-long Russian strikes across six districts of Kyiv early Sunday 19 July killed one person and wounded at least 16, in what Ukrainian officials called the largest ballistic-missile attack on the capital since the war began.
story.display_body: Russia hit Kyiv with what Ukrainian officials called the largest ballistic-missile barrage on the capital since the war began, early Sunday 19 July. The hours-long attack spanned six districts of the city, killing one person and wounding at least 16, Ukraine's national police said. It came a day after a Ukrainian drone attack struck e-commerce warehouses near Moscow — the latest exchange in an intensifying summer air campaign. Al Jazeera noted Kyiv is weathering near-daily strikes amid a shortage of US-made Patriot interceptors (its best defence against ballistic missiles).
story.why: Even with a comparatively low death toll, a record ballistic-missile salvo underscores how thinly stretched Ukraine's air defences are as Western interceptor stocks run low.

fields.display_body: Russia hit Kyiv with what Ukrainian officials called the largest ballistic-missile barrage on the capital since the war began, early Sunday 19 July. Al Jazeera noted Kyiv is weathering near-daily strikes amid a shortage of US-made Patriot interceptors (its best defence against ballistic missiles).
fields.why: Even with a comparatively low death toll, a record ballistic-missile salvo underscores how thinly stretched Ukraine's air defences are as Western interceptor stocks run low.

story.headline: At least 50 Malian soldiers killed in Tuareg-jihadist attack
story.summary: At least 50 Malian soldiers were killed and dozens captured in a coordinated attack by Tuareg separatists and allied jihadists, local sources told AFP.
story.display_body: At least 50 Malian soldiers were killed and dozens more captured in a coordinated attack by Tuareg separatists and allied jihadists, reported Sunday 19 July. Local sources told AFP that the assault — one of the deadliest single blows to Mali's army in its grinding northern insurgency — overran an army position, with dozens of troops taken prisoner. The Tuareg are a Saharan ethnic group whose rebels have long fought the government in Bamako, at times in alliance with jihadist factions.
story.why: The toll signals that Mali's junta-led government and its Russian security partners are still losing ground in the Sahel, a region whose instability drives migration toward Europe.

fields.display_body: At least 50 Malian soldiers were killed and dozens more captured in a coordinated attack by Tuareg separatists and allied jihadists, reported Sunday 19 July. story.headline: Hungary's president signs the constitutional reform that ends his own mandate
story.summary: President Tamas Sulyok signed on 18 July the constitutional amendment terminating his own presidency, following parliament's 139-6 vote to oust him, warning it damages the rule of law.
story.display_body: Hungary's president, Tamas Sulyok, signed on Saturday 18 July the constitutional amendment that ends his own mandate, effective Sunday 19 July. The move followed parliament's 13 July vote (139–6) to oust him, driven by Peter Magyar's opposition Tisza party, which now commands a two-thirds majority; the package also caps the terms of constitutional judges and members of parliament. Sulyok, himself a former constitutional judge, said he had no choice but to approve the law but warned that it inflicts serious damage on the rule of law and the separation of powers and sets a dangerous precedent.
story.why: A sitting head of state enacting his own removal is an extraordinary constitutional moment, and for a Swiss and EU reader it sharpens the question of Hungary's institutional direction as an anti-Orban opposition consolidates power.

story.headline: Swiss authorities warn of 'violent nihilism' as a new form of youth extremism
story.summary: Swiss security authorities are warning about a spreading, ideology-free form of youth radicalization that glorifies violence for its own sake.
story.display_body: Swiss security authorities are warning of a new, ideology-free form of youth radicalization they call "violent nihilism," SRF reported Sunday 19 July. Unlike conventional extremism, the movement advances neither a political nor a religious cause; according to the authorities, it glorifies violence for its own sake, drawing vulnerable young people into loose online networks. The warning frames it as a distinct and spreading phenomenon among Swiss adolescents rather than a variant of known jihadist or far-right radicalization.
story.why: An online-native extremism with no political or religious programme is harder for existing counter-radicalization efforts — built around identifiable ideologies — to detect and disrupt.

story.headline: Switzerland's birth decline is driven by fear of the third child
story.summary: University of Lausanne demographer Laura Bernardi says Switzerland's falling fertility is unusually driven by couples forgoing a third child rather than a first, unlike most EU states.
story.display_body: Switzerland's falling birth rate is unusually driven by couples forgoing a third child, not by skipping parenthood, University of Lausanne demographer Laura Bernardi told SRF (published Sunday 19 July). Whereas in most EU states the decline is mainly explained by fewer first births, in Switzerland only about half of the drop is; the rest, Bernardi says, comes from families stopping at two children. For scale, SRF notes South Korea — where a hyper-competitive environment pushes the young to forgo children or stop at one — has the world's lowest fertility rate, around 0.7 children per woman.
story.why: It reframes the policy lever, suggesting that supporting larger families, not just first-time parents, would do more to lift Swiss fertility.
